1: Vayiqach Qorach: 'Now Korah took [men].' The verb laqach (take) without a direct object is grammatically anomalous — the text never states clearly what Korah took. Ancient readers noticed this; the LXX attempts to smooth it. The force may be: 'Korah took himself [and his followers]' — he took initiative, he seized the moment. Korah is a Kohathite (son of Izhar son of Kohath son of Levi) — the sub-clan that carries the most holy objects of the tabernacle, including the ark. He already occupies the most prestigious Levitical role; he wants more: the priesthood itself.
2: The coalition against Moses has two components: (1) Korah with his Levitical supporters (those seeking the priesthood); (2) Dathan and Abiram, Reubenites (who have a political grievance — Reuben is Jacob's firstborn and has been displaced). 250 leaders of the congregation (qetziney ha'edah) join them — men of renown (anshey shem). The rebellion is not a mob action but a coordinated challenge by the most prominent members of the community. This makes the judgment that follows especially severe.
3: rav lachem ki kol ha'edah kulam qedoshim: 'You have gone too far! The whole congregation is holy, every one of them.' This is the theological claim of the rebellion: if YHWH's spirit rests on all Israel (a point Moses himself affirms in Numbers 11:29), what justifies the distinction between Levite and priest? The argument has a surface plausibility. The answer Numbers 16 gives is not that the claim is wrong (Exodus 19:6 does call Israel a kingdom of priests) but that it is deployed in the wrong direction: the congregation's holiness does not eliminate YHWH's prerogative to appoint specific mediators.
4: vayipol al panav: 'he fell on his face.' Moses's response to the challenge is not counter-argument but prostration — he brings the case immediately to YHWH. The same response appears at the golden calf (Exodus 32:11-12) and at the spies' report (Numbers 14:5). Moses's pattern under pressure is intercession, not self-defense. This itself is the answer to Korah's charge: a man who responds to opposition by lying face-down before God is not an autocrat claiming privileges for himself.
5: boqer veyoda YHWH et asher lo: 'In the morning YHWH will show who is his.' The word yoda (will show/make known) is the Hiphil of yadah (know) — YHWH will make known through the ordeal what he already knows. Moses proposes the censer-test: let each claimant bring incense before YHWH and YHWH will show whom he has chosen. This is judicial self-restraint — Moses does not assert his own authority but defers to divine demonstration.
6: The censer test: each man takes a machtah (censer/fire pan) — the same instrument Nadab and Abihu used when they offered strange fire (Leviticus 10:1) and the same instrument that the 250 authorized officiants of the tabernacle ceremony use. The invitation to bring incense is simultaneously an invitation to judgment: the correct use of the censer, by the correctly authorized person, is worship; the incorrect use brings death. Moses essentially says: bring your best case before YHWH and let him decide.
7: rav lachem beney Levi: 'You have gone too far, sons of Levi!' Moses turns Korah's own charge back on him: it is the rebels who have gone too far (rav lachem), not Moses and Aaron. The formal chiasm between v3 (Korah: 'You have gone too far, all the congregation is holy') and v7b (Moses: 'You have gone too far, sons of Levi') is a deliberate literary reversal. Moses exposes that the rebellion is not about the congregation's holiness but about Korah's ambition.
8: Moses addresses Korah and his fellow Levites directly. The appeal to 'listen now, sons of Levi' (shim'u na beney Levi) uses the particle na (please/now) — a mark of respectful entreaty rather than command. Moses does not shout; he reasons with the rebels before the divine judgment falls. This deliberate appeal-before-judgment is the procedure Paul identifies as the prophetic calling: 'We implore you on behalf of Christ, be reconciled to God' (2 Corinthians 5:20).
9: hame'at mikem ki hivdil Elohey Yisrael etchem me'adat Yisrael: 'Is it too small a thing for you that the God of Israel has separated you from the congregation of Israel?' The rhetorical question exposes the ingratitude at the core of the rebellion. YHWH has already elevated the Levites above the congregation for sanctuary service — a privilege Korah treats as insufficient. Hivdil (to separate, Hiphil of badal) is the verb of consecration and distinction: YHWH's own creative act of separation (Genesis 1:4, 6) is applied to the Levites' privileged calling.
10: utevaqqeshu gam kehunah: 'And yet you seek the priesthood too!' The accusation is direct: Korah seeks both the Levitical privilege (which he already has) and the priestly privilege (which is Aaronic). Tvaqqeshu (you seek/demand) is covetous reaching beyond one's calling. Moses's point is that YHWH has already given the Levites more than the congregation receives — the demand for more is not championing equality but coveting a different status altogether.
11: lachen atah vekhol adatcha hano'adim al YHWH: 'Therefore it is you and all your company who have gathered against YHWH.' The rebellion is reframed in its ultimate dimension: this is not a grievance against Moses and Aaron but against YHWH himself. Moses asks the rhetorical question that devastates Korah's argument: 'What is Aaron that you grumble against him?' Aaron holds his office by divine appointment, not personal ambition — to grumble against Aaron is to grumble against the God who appointed him.
12: Moses summons Dathan and Abiram, who refuse to appear: 'We will not come up.' The refusal itself is an act of rebellion — a summons from Moses is a summons from YHWH's appointed leader. The Reubenites' grievance (v13-14) is different from Korah's: not priestly ambition but political resentment — Moses has 'brought them out of a land flowing with milk and honey' (ironically calling Egypt the promised land) and 'has not brought them into a land flowing with milk and honey.'
13: ha'at ki he'elitanu me'eretz zavat chalav udevash lahamitenu bamidbar: 'Is it too small a thing that you have brought us up out of a land flowing with milk and honey to kill us in the wilderness?' The bitter irony: Egypt is called the land flowing with milk and honey — the precise designation of Canaan. The inversion is deliberate mockery of Moses's language, exposing how deeply the failure to enter Canaan (Num 13-14) has poisoned the mood of the community. Dathan and Abiram have adopted the rebellious perspective of the spy-report: the promised land is unattainable and the exodus was a mistake.
14: af lo el eretz zavat chalav udevash heviatanu: 'You have not brought us into a land flowing with milk and honey.' The charge is factually correct at this moment in the narrative — but it ignores the reason: the congregation's own refusal (Num 14). Dathan and Abiram accuse Moses of failure while suppressing the community's role in that failure. The phrase 'will you put out the eyes of these men?' (haeynot ha'anashim hahem tenaqqer) is obscure but seems to mean: 'Do you think you can blind us to the truth?' — an accusation of deliberate deception.
15: wayichar leMoshe me'od: 'Then Moses was very angry.' The only clear expression of Moses's personal anger in this episode — prompted by Dathan and Abiram's contemptuous refusal and false charges. His prayer to YHWH 'do not accept their offering' (al tepen el minchatam) invokes the same language Cain feared (Genesis 4:5): the withdrawal of divine acceptance. Moses then makes his own record: 'I have not taken one donkey from them, nor have I harmed any of them' — a selfless leader defending his integrity.
16: Moses instructs Korah: the test will happen tomorrow at the tabernacle — Korah and his 250 men vs. Aaron, all bringing censers. The symmetry of the test is notable: Korah claims that all the congregation are equally holy; the ordeal will demonstrate whether all 251 men (Korah + 250 + Aaron) are equally holy, or whether Aaron's election is unique. The test is fair by its own logic: if Korah is right, his censer will be accepted; if Aaron is right, the 250 will die.
17: Each of the 251 men brings his censer — 250 for the challengers, one for Aaron. The number 250 is specific and will be recalled at v36-40 when the censers become a memorial plating. The gathering of this many leaders with incense censers at the tabernacle entrance is the most public possible confrontation — the entire congregation is watching (v19). The drama is at maximum before the verdict falls.
18: The 250 men stand at the entrance of the tent of meeting with their censers burning — the same physical position Aaron and his sons occupy during authorized priestly service. From the outside, the scene looks like a liturgical ceremony; from the inside, it is a challenge to the divine prerogative. YHWH's glory appears to the whole congregation precisely at this moment (v19), giving the judgment maximum visibility.
19: vayiqahel aleihem Qorach: 'Korah gathered the whole congregation against them.' Korah has successfully rallied the entire community to his side — the same congregation that will need to be kept alive through the plague that follows. YHWH's glory (kevod YHWH) appearing 'to the whole congregation' is the dramatic pivot: before anyone dies, YHWH visually manifests. This is the last moment the congregation might repent.
20: YHWH commands Moses and Aaron to 'separate yourselves from this congregation so that I may consume them in an instant' (ke'raga = in a moment, in the blink of an eye). The immediacy of the divine threat is absolute — not a conditional warning but an impending execution. The fact that YHWH warns Moses and Aaron first, before acting, reflects the pattern of Num 14: YHWH allows time for intercession even when judgment is imminent.
21: hibadelu mitoch ha'edah hazot va'achaleh otam keraga: 'Separate yourselves from this congregation so that I may consume them in an instant.' The verb badal (separate) appears again — YHWH had separated the Levites for service (v9); now he separates Moses and Aaron from the community before destroying it. The irony is sharp: Korah challenged the separations YHWH made; YHWH now makes a sharper separation — between the condemned and the saved.
22: El Elohey harukhot lekhol basar: 'O God, the God of the spirits of all flesh.' This invocation — unique in the Torah — addresses YHWH as the source of the animating spirit in every living person. Moses and Aaron's intercession is grounded in the divine responsibility for all life: 'Shall one man sin, and will you be angry with the whole congregation?' The rhetorical question makes the case for the congregation's corporate innocence — not all of them conspired with Korah. YHWH accepts the logic and focuses the judgment.
23: YHWH's response to the intercession is precise rather than total: instead of consuming the whole congregation, he tells Moses to have the community separate from the specific tents of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram. The surgical narrowing of the judgment from 'whole congregation' to 'specific households' is the direct result of Moses and Aaron's intercession. Intercession changes the scope of the judgment.
24: Korah Datan va'Aviram: The three named leaders are the specific targets of the earth's opening. The congregation must move away from their dwelling-tents. The divine instruction to separate creates a spatial boundary that simultaneously protects the innocent and marks the guilty for judgment. The act of moving away from their tents is itself a public declaration of whose side one is on.
25: Moses goes personally to Dathan and Abiram's tents, accompanied by the elders of Israel. The elders who follow Moses (despite the general grumbling) are the remnant who retain their loyalty. Moses goes to where the stubborn rebels are rather than waiting for them to come to the tabernacle — a last pastoral attempt to reach men who refused his summons (v12).
26: suru na me'al ohaley ha'anashim harashah'im ha'eleh: 'Move away from the tents of these wicked men.' Moses publicly names Dathan and Abiram as resha'im (wicked ones) before the assembly — the first direct condemnation in this episode. 'Touch nothing of theirs, lest you be swept away in all their sins' — the contagion of sin is treated as physical: proximity to the condemned creates risk of being caught in their judgment. The congregation's obedience here is the same separating-from-the-condemned pattern that the NT applies to the church's handling of unrepentant sin (1 Corinthians 5:11).
27: The congregation separates; Dathan and Abiram emerge from their tents and stand at the entrance with their wives, sons, and small children — their whole households. The families' presence at the moment of judgment is both terrible and theologically significant: the rebellion's consequence falls on the household, not just the individuals. Ancient corporate identity means the household is implicated in the leader's choices. This is why the text will later note that Korah's children did not die (Numbers 26:11) — YHWH makes a specific exception within the household solidarity principle.
28: bezo't tede'un ki YHWH shelachani: 'By this you shall know that YHWH has sent me.' Moses stakes his prophetic credibility on the outcome of the judgment: if these men die a natural death, YHWH has not sent Moses; if the ground does something unprecedented, YHWH has spoken through his prophet. This prophetic self-testing is remarkable — Moses does not pray for the miracle to happen; he simply declares that if YHWH acts, his own mission is confirmed. The 'sign-condition' is the most concentrated expression of prophetic authentication in Numbers.
29: The conditional: if they die naturally (kemet kol ha'adam = the common death of all mankind) or receive a normal visitation (fequdat kol ha'adam), then YHWH has not sent Moses. The stakes are absolute. Moses is not hedging — he is making a public, falsifiable claim about divine authorization. The 'natural death' option being explicitly excluded means the miraculous judgment that follows is unambiguous as divine action.
30: im beriah yivra YHWH: 'But if YHWH creates something entirely new (beriah).' The word beriah (new creation) comes from the root bara' (create), used of God's creation in Genesis 1:1. A ground-swallowing judgment would be an act of creation — as unprecedented as the original creation. The phrase anticipates Isaiah 43:19 ('I am about to do a new thing') and the NT's language of the new creation in Christ. Moses calls for a miracle using the vocabulary of original creation.
31: vatibqa ha'adamah asher tachteihem: 'the ground under them split apart.' The immediacy is total — Moses finishes speaking and the earth opens. The narrative compression is deliberate: Moses's word and YHWH's act are virtually simultaneous, confirming that Moses spoke as YHWH's prophet. The splitting ground is the most visceral possible judgment — the earth itself becomes YHWH's instrument of execution.
32: vatiftach ha'aretz et piha vatival otam: 'The earth opened its mouth and swallowed them up.' The anthropomorphic image — the earth with a mouth — depicts judgment as total, final, and irreversible. Not killed, not struck by plague, but swallowed whole: they disappear from the surface of the created world. 'Along with their households, all the people belonging to Korah, and all their possessions' — the comprehensive catalog of loss emphasizes the totality of the destruction.
33: vayerdu hem vekhol asher lahem chayim she'olah: 'They went down alive into Sheol.' The phrase chayim (alive) is the opposite of the normal death trajectory — people go to Sheol after dying, not while still living. This is the most extreme form of the 'cut off' covenant judgment: not just death but annihilation, disappearance from both the living and the dead — entered the realm of the dead without passing through normal death. 'The earth closed over them, and they perished from the midst of the assembly.'
34: The surrounding Israelites flee at the sound of the condemned men's cries — 'lest the earth swallow us also!' The fear is both understandable and a warning to future generations: proximity to YHWH's judgment is dangerous. The fleeing congregation enacts the separation YHWH commanded (v24) through sheer terror. Their flight creates the visual image of the condemned standing alone as the earth opens under them.
35: ve'esh yatse'ah me'et YHWH vatochal et hashamosh: 'Fire came out from YHWH and consumed the 250 men who were offering incense.' The fire from YHWH executing the 250 censers-bearers echoes the fire that consumed Nadab and Abihu (Leviticus 10:2 — the same formula, almost verbatim). The parallel is intentional: both episodes involve unauthorized incense-offering at the tabernacle; both result in divine fire. The connection establishes the pattern: YHWH's fire is the judgment for those who approach the sanctuary without authorization.
36: A new divine speech (v36-40) addresses the disposition of the censers. The 250 censers belong to the sanctuary — they were used in service before YHWH (even in unauthorized service) and are therefore holy. They cannot simply be discarded; they must be repurposed in a way that memorializes the judgment.
37: Eleazar (not Aaron — Aaron is kept away from corpse-impurity, Leviticus 21:11-12) is to take the censers from among the burning men and scatter the coals. The coals themselves are to be scattered away — they were the unauthorized fire, and the fire of YHWH has rendered them dangerous. The censers, however, have become holy through contact with YHWH's judgment-fire and must be treated accordingly.
38: et machtot hachat'aim ha'eleh benafashotam: 'the censers of these men who sinned at the cost of their own lives.' The phrase benafashotam (with/at the cost of their lives) captures the self-destructive nature of their sin: they used their censers as weapons against their own lives. The censers are to be hammered into plating (riqui'ay pechim) for the altar — bronze from the condemned men's censers becomes the facing of the altar of burnt offering. The judgment is permanently inscribed in the altar's surface.
39: Eleazar carries out the command: the 250 bronze censers are hammered into altar plating. The same altar at which Korah's men stood with their unauthorized incense now bears the memorial of their judgment on its surface. Every subsequent authorized incense-offering at the altar is performed against the backdrop of the censer-plating reminder: unauthorized approach to YHWH's altar is lethal.
40: zikaron libeney Yisrael lema'an asher lo yiqrav ish zar: 'a reminder to the Israelites that no unauthorized person — not of Aaron's line — should come near to offer incense before YHWH.' The word zikaron (memorial/reminder) is the same term used for the memorial stones at the Jordan (Joshua 4:7) and the Passover memorial (Exodus 12:14). The censer-plating is not decorative but didactic: a permanent visual warning inscribed on the most public object in the sanctuary. The phrase 'as YHWH directed him through Moses' closes the unit with the divine-authority frame.
41: The most astonishing verse in the chapter: the day after the earth-swallowing and the fire, 'the whole congregation of Israel grumbled against Moses and Aaron, saying: You have killed the people of YHWH.' The rebels who were destroyed are now called 'the people of YHWH' — the congregation inverts the divine verdict, treating the judged as victims and Moses and Aaron as murderers. This re-grumbling demonstrates the hard-heartedness that required the full generation to die in the wilderness (Numbers 14:29-35). Judgment does not automatically produce repentance.
42: As the congregation assembles against Moses and Aaron, the cloud covers the tent of meeting and YHWH's glory appears — the same theophany as in v19. YHWH's visible presence appears precisely when the congregation threatens Moses and Aaron, constituting a divine protection. Moses and Aaron turn toward the tent — the proper posture for receiving divine communication — as the glory is about to speak.
43: Moses and Aaron go to the front of the tent of meeting — between the approaching congregation and the divine glory, exactly the mediatorial position their calling requires. The same spatial arrangement that existed throughout the Korah episode (Moses and Aaron between YHWH and the people) is reproduced. They are the buffer zone between divine holiness and human rebellion.
44: YHWH speaks again with the same command as v21: 'Get away from this congregation so that I may consume them in an instant.' The repetition of the threat (same words, same immediacy) marks the escalation: the first judgment fell on the specifically wicked; this judgment threatens the entire congregation, which has compounded the sin by accusing Moses and Aaron of murder. The re-grumbling is treated as a new and greater rebellion.
45: Moses and Aaron fall facedown again — the third time in the chapter (v4, v22, v45) Moses prostrates himself under pressure. The threefold prostration marks Moses as the supreme intercessor in Numbers: every time YHWH threatens destruction, Moses's first response is to fall before YHWH. The plague has already begun (hachel hanegef = 'the plague has begun') — YHWH acts while Moses and Aaron are still prostrate.
46: Moshe el Aharon qach et hamachtah: Moses instructs Aaron to take his censer, take coals from the altar, add incense, and run into the middle of the congregation to make atonement (kapper). This is the most concentrated atonement act in Numbers: the high priest runs with burning incense into the midst of a plague-struck crowd. The action uses the same censer that Korah's men used illegitimately — but Aaron is the authorized priest, using authorized fire, in an act of authorized intercession.
47: vayarots Aharon el toch haqqahal: 'Aaron ran into the middle of the congregation.' The verb ruts (run) signals urgency — Aaron did not walk ceremonially but ran. This running high priest, incense burning, moves toward the dying in order to stand between them and the plague. The physical action embodies the theological function of priesthood: moving toward death in order to stop it, interposing oneself as the atoning mediator between YHWH's judgment and the people's lives.
48: vayaamod beyn hamaytim uveyn hachayim vate'atsar hamaggefah: 'He stood between the dead and the living, and the plague was stopped.' The spatial description of Aaron — literally between the corpses and the living survivors — is the most vivid image of priestly mediation in the entire Torah. The plague stops at the point where the authorized atonement-smoke rises. The Levitical high priest physically occupying the boundary between death and life is the enacted image of what Christ does as the mediator: standing between humanity's death-sentence and YHWH's life.
49: The death toll: 14,700 — in addition to those who died in the Korah incident itself. The precision of the number (not 'thousands' but 14,700) marks this as a historical record. The plague's speed is implied by v47-48: it had 'already begun' and was stopped by Aaron's running intercession. The gap between 'already begun' and Aaron arriving suggests the 14,700 died in a very short time — YHWH's judgment is both swift and stoppable by authorized intercession.
50: The chapter closes with Aaron returning to Moses at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the plague had been stopped. The closing scene reunites the two brothers at the sanctuary threshold after Aaron's solo run into the dying crowd. The literary frame of the chapter (Moses and Aaron at the tabernacle entrance, v18, v43, v50) holds the entire rebellion-and-plague narrative within the tent-of-meeting context: all of it happens within YHWH's visible presence.
